title: Customer Service and Production Workflow Design

blocks.html:
A customer service agent juggles tools, policy, emotion, and risk at once. Its job description in one sentence: resolve what it can, escalate when it should, and communicate uncertainty honestly.

Escalation is about the nature of the situation, never a counter. Escalate when the user asks for a human, when the issue needs authority the agent lacks, when a policy exception or high-value transaction appears, when progress stalls, or when a tool reports an uncertain or unsafe state. "Escalate after three failed tools" is blind to all of these — one uncertain payment write should escalate instantly; five harmless read timeouts shouldn't.

And when you do escalate, hand off structured context: who, what issue, the root cause found, record IDs, amounts, what's already been done, and a recommended next action. The human should start where the agent stopped — not where the customer started.

Policy: store credits over $200 need manager approval. The broken design puts the rule in the prompt and gives the tool an approved_by_manager flag — which the model itself sets. The audit finds exactly what you'd expect. The correct design removes the flag from the interface entirely: the tool reads the threshold from a server-controlled policy source, disburses below it, and above it returns a structured requires_approval result that routes to a manager. The model cannot bypass a parameter that doesn't exist. For the heaviest actions, add preview-then-execute with a short-lived, payload-bound, single-use token — and have every tool re-verify the caller's authority on every call. "The model already checked policy" is not a defense.

The human-experience patterns:

  • Frustrated user demanding a human, but the fix is instant: acknowledge, then offer both — "I can resolve this now, and I can also transfer you." Never silently act after they asked for a person; never run a long intake questionnaire.
  • Graceful degradation: when a tool fails mid-workflow, report what's verified, what's pending, and how to finish. Users tolerate visible incompleteness; they do not tolerate discovering a "completed" action silently never happened.
  • Repeated identical failure is a signal to switch strategies — different tool, clarifying question, or escalation — not to burn more retries.
  • Defense-in-depth: prompt rules bias the agent, tool implementations enforce the policy, audit logs detect what slipped through. All three layers, because each covers the others' gaps.

items: Escalate on situation category and impact, never on failure counters.
Handoffs carry structure: root cause, records, actions taken, recommended next step.
Hard policy lives inside tools with server-controlled thresholds — no model-settable approval flags.
Partial completion: say what's done, what's pending, what's next — never claim unfinished side effects.
Defense-in-depth: prompts bias, tools enforce, audits detect.
